revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Hakushu (Shinto)]][[ja:拍手 (神道)]][[ru:Касивадэ]][[simple:Hakushu]][[zh:拍手 (神道)]]
{{Short description|Clapping in Shinto}} [[File:参拝作法_2016_(28818026392).jpg|alt=神社の参拝方法についての案内板。日本語と英語。|thumb|Worshiping manners, 2016, Nagoya, Japan. The etiquette of {{ill|Two bows, two claps, one bow|en|Two bows, two claps, one bow|qid=Q86725413|lt=Two bows, two claps, one bow|1=Two bows, two claps, one bow}} is explained in both Japanese and English.]] [[File:Bowing-clapping-bowing.png|alt=|thumb|502x502px|An example of prewar two-beat, one-beat worship. The upper row is the second worship, the middle row is the second clap, and the lower row is the first worship. This is the worship after offering the tamagushi, and the tamagushi can be seen on the table in front. Source: NDLJP:1054789/27.]] '''Hakushu''' {{lang|ja|拍手 (神道)}} is a word used to refer to ceremonial clapping in [[Shinto]].<ref>{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=8972 |access-date=2023-03-11 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}}</ref> It is also known as '''Kashiwade'''.
It is a part of the {{ill|two bows, two claps, one bow|ja|二礼二拍手一礼|simple|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/two bows, two claps, one bow|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Two bows, two claps, one bow|qid=Q86725413}} practice done when praying at a shrine in Japan.<ref>https://archive.today/20230408005036/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9055</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=How many times should you bow and clap at the shrine? (Tips #2) |url=https://akemisagawa.typepad.com/my-blog/2016/05/how-many-times-should-you-bow-and-clap-at-the-shrine-tips-2.html |access-date=2023-04-08 |website=Akemi's Blog}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |last=D |first=John |date=2019-02-17 |title=First steps in Shinto |url=https://www.greenshinto.com/2019/02/17/first-steps-in-shinto/ |access-date=2023-04-08 |website=Green Shinto |language=en-GB}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=The Basics of Shrine Visiting! Must-Know Information and Some Recommended Shrines in Japan |url=https://www.the-kansai-guide.com/en/article/item/16342/ |access-date=2023-04-08 |website=The KANSAI Guide - The Origin of Japan, KANSAI |date=19 October 2021 |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{cite thesis |id={{ProQuest|1925951527}} |last1=Rodrigue |first1=Craig E |date=2017 |title=American Shinto Community of Practice: Community Formation outside Original Context }}{{pn|date=November 2023}}</ref>
Ceremonial clapping is quiet and soft.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Kashiwade (Ceremonial Clapping |url=https://issuu.com/charliesan763/docs/how_to_draw_manga_vol._11_maids___miko/s/11610807 |access-date=2023-02-16 |website=issuu |language=en}}{{rs|date=November 2023}}{{self-published inline|date=November 2023}}</ref>
It is considered to have a Buddhist meaning with the right hand meaning Buddha and the left hand meaning all beings so unification of Buddha and all beings.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Why are the hands clapped when praying at shrines (Shintoism) and placed together when praying at temples (Buddhism)? Online Atlas English school. Teaching jobs in Osaka,Sapporo,Yokohama,Nagoya,Kyoto,Kobe |url=http://atlasp.net/questions/4.html |access-date=2023-02-12 |website=atlasp.net}}</ref> As [[Shinbutsu-shūgō|Buddhism and shinto have been together for so long]] it is unclear which one it originated from.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2013-04-14 |title=Why do some Japanese people clap their hands when they pray for something at the temple? |url=https://travelience.com/faq2/why-some-japanese-people-are-clapping-hands-when-they-wish-something-at-the-temple/ |access-date=2023-02-12 |website=GoWithGuide by Travelience |language=en}}</ref>
The practice is present in {{ill|Aikido|en|Aikido|qid=Q43114|lt=Aikido|1=Aikido}}<ref>{{Cite web |title=Hakushu - Culturesmith |url=https://culturesmith.com/Hakushu |access-date=2023-02-16 |website=culturesmith.com}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |last=dontmakemeangrymrmcgee |date=2015-07-06 |title=Shinto Clapping and Aikido. |url=https://dontmakemeangrymrmcgee.wordpress.com/2015/07/06/shinto-clapping-and-aikido/ |access-date=2023-02-16 |website=dontmakemeangrymrmcgee |language=en}}</ref> and {{ill|Bujinkan|en|Bujinkan|qid=Q851493|lt=Bujinkan|1=Bujinkan}}.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2011-07-01 |title=拍手 Hakushu: The Sound of Ninjas Clapping ? |url=https://bujinkangard.wordpress.com/2011/07/01/%e6%8b%8d%e6%89%8b-hakushu-the-sound-of-ninjas-clapping/ |access-date=2023-02-16 |website=INFOS BUJINKAN |language=en}}</ref>
== History == Hakushu has a long history in Shinto.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=8972 |access-date=2023-03-11 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}}</ref> The {{ill|Wajinden|en|Wajinden|qid=Q1135798|lt=Wajinden|1=Wajinden}} describes the people of the {{ill|Yayoi period|en|Yayoi period|qid=Q207858|lt=Yayoi period|1=Yayoi period}} as clapping in worship.<ref>https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/%E9%AD%8F%E5%BF%97%E5%80%AD%E4%BA%BA%E4%BC%9D</ref>
Some people considered it as Buddhist, with the right hand meaning Buddha and the left hand meaning all beings so unification of Buddha and all beings.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Why are the hands clapped when praying at shrines (Shintoism) and placed together when praying at temples (Buddhism)? Online Atlas english school. Teaching jobs in Osaka,Sapporo,Yokohama,Nagoya,Kyoto,Kobe |url=http://atlasp.net/questions/4.html |access-date=2023-02-12 |website=atlasp.net}}</ref> As [[Shinbutsu-shūgō|Buddhism and shinto have been together for so long]] it is unclear which one it originated from.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2013-04-14 |title=Why do some Japanese people clap their hands when they pray for something at the temple? |url=https://travelience.com/faq2/why-some-japanese-people-are-clapping-hands-when-they-wish-something-at-the-temple/ |access-date=2023-02-12 |website=GoWithGuide by Travelience |language=en}}</ref>
However the origin of the modern "{{ill|Two bows, two claps, one bow|en|Two bows, two claps, one bow|qid=Q86725413|lt=Two bows, two claps, one bow|1=Two bows, two claps, one bow}}" etiquette can be traced back to the "Shrine Festival" in 1873, where it was written as "revisit applause." Over time, various methods were devised and improved, leading to the creation of the current etiquette. The "Shrine Rituals and Events Etiquette" enacted in the 40th year of the Meiji era defined the etiquette as "returning, clapping twice, pressing together, praying, praying, clapping twice, and bowing again."<ref name="yoriso">{{Cite web |title=二礼二拍一礼は参拝の基本。その歴史と考え方とは。｜葬儀･家族葬なら【よりそうお葬式】 |url=https://www.yoriso.com/sogi/nireinihakuichirei/ |access-date=2023-02-11 |website=www.yoriso.com |language=ja}}</ref> However, at [[Usa Jingū]] and [[Izumo-taisha]], it is correct etiquette to bow twice, clap ''four'' times, then bow once.<ref> [http://www.usajinguu.com/worship/ "Usa Jingu - About Worship"] retrieved May 31 2024. </ref><ref> [https://izumooyashiro.or.jp/archives/faq/7898 "Izumo-Taisha - Frequently Asked Questions"] retrieved May 31 2024.</ref>
It is widely accepted that "two bows, two claps, one bow" is the formal etiquette of worship for the chief priest. The etiquette was created through the long experience and improvement of the predecessors and is a reflection of the respect for the gods.<ref name="yoriso" />
== See also == * {{ill|Tejime|en|Tejime|qid=Q3982616|lt=Tejime|1=Tejime}} * {{ill|Institute of Divinities|en|Institute of Divinities|qid=Q11590709|lt=Institute of Divinities|1=Institute of Divinities}}
== References == <references />
{{shinto shrines}}
